Chapter 17
Ethers, Epoxides, and Sulfides
©Evgeniy Ivanao/Getty Image: ©Bill grove/Getty image
© 2020 McGraw-Hill Education. All rights reserved. Authorized only for instructor use in the classroom. No reproduction or further distribution permitted without the prior written consent of McGraw-Hill Education.

Ethers, Epoxides, and Sulfides
Ethers (ROR) are relatively unreactive; their most important reactivity is the Lewis basicity of the oxygen atom
Epoxides are an important exception to this rule
Sulfides (RSR), the sulfur analogues of ethers, have different physical properties and reactivity

Section 17.1
NOMENCLATURE OF ETHERS, EPOXIDES, AND SULFIDES

Nomenclature of Ethers
Ethers can be named as alkoxyalkanes (substitutive) or as alkyl alkyl’ ethers (functional class )
Ethers with identical R groups are symmetrical ; ethers with different R groups are unsymmetrical
Cyclic ethers have unique names; the oxygen is treated as position 1

Nomenclature of Sulfides
Sulfides can be named using substitutive (alkylthio-) or functional class (alkyl alkyl’ sulfide) nomenclature
Sulfur analogues of epoxides are called thiiranes

Section 17.2
STRUCTURE AND BONDING IN ETHERS AND EPOXIDES

Structure and Bonding
The oxygen in alkyl ethers is sp 3 hybridized, but the typical bond angle is wider than 109.5 degrees due to strain
The ether oxygen behaves largely like a CH 2 unit from a conformational perspective

Physical Properties of Ethers
Ether molecules, unlike alcohols, lack the ability to hydrogen bond with themselves
Boiling and melting points are similar to alkanes of comparable size
Ethers can hydrogen bond with H-bond donors such as water
Solubility of ethers in water is generally high as a result

Crown Ethers: Applied Basicity
The most important principle of reactivity for ethers is that the ether oxygen can act as a Lewis base
Cyclic polyethers have multiple oxygens that can bind ( chelate ) to a Lewis acid at multiple points
Crown ethers are cyclic polyethers of various sizes that bind various cations

Complexation of Cations
The Lewis basic cavity of a crown ether provides a “pocket” where cations can bind
For example, addition of 18-crown-6 increases the solubility of KF in benzene, a nonpolar solvent
The hydrophobic outer edge is solvated by benzene
The Lewis basic inner core binds K +

Preparation of Ethers
Symmetrical ethers can be prepared via acid-catalyzed condensation of alcohols, a substitution process
Ethers can also be prepared via addition of O and H across C=C, acid-catalyzed hydration

Williamson Ether Synthesis
Alkoxides ( RO − ) are great nucleophiles; reaction of RO − with an alkyl halide RX forms a new C–O bond
This is an attractive method for the synthesis of ethers !
To avoid E2 elimination, the alkyl halide should be primary
The substitution of the alkoxide is largely irrelevant
